original_title: Expressing opposition to Central Business District Tolling Program of New York City.
plain_title: Congress Opposes New York City's Toll for Driving into Manhattan
plain_summary: This resolution is Congress officially saying they disagree with New York City's plan to charge drivers a fee (called congestion pricing) to enter the busiest part of Manhattan. The goal of NYC's program is to reduce traffic and pollution by making it more expensive to drive downtown. Congress is pushing for the program to be paused and studied more before it moves forward.
key_provisions: Officially opposes New York City's congestion pricing toll program
Calls for a study on how the tolls would affect the local economy
Urges federal agencies and New York to stop putting the program into effect
who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects drivers who commute into or travel through lower and midtown Manhattan, as well as New York City businesses and residents who depend on traffic flow in that area.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to a House committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

plain_title: A Bill With No Details or Summary Yet Available
plain_summary: This bill has been reserved for the Speaker of the House, meaning a spot has been saved for it but no actual content has been written or released yet. Think of it like a placeholder, similar to saving a seat at lunch before you know who is sitting there. No one outside of leadership knows what this bill will be about yet.
key_provisions: No provisions available yet
Bill content has not been released
Details are pending future announcement
who_it_affects: It is currently unknown who this bill would affect because no content or summary has been made public yet.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder right now and has not been introduced, debated, or voted on yet.

plain_summary: This bill has been reserved by the Speaker of the House, meaning a spot has been saved for it but no actual content has been written or released yet. Think of it like a placeholder in a notebook where someone wrote 'save this page' but hasn't filled it in. There is no information yet about what this bill would do.
key_provisions: No provisions available yet

status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder right now and has no content, votes, or any recorded actions yet.

plain_title: A Bill Held Open for the Minority Leader to Name
plain_summary: This is essentially a placeholder bill — a spot in the legislative lineup that has been set aside for the Minority Leader of Congress to use. The Minority Leader is the head of whichever political party has fewer seats in that chamber. No actual policy or rules have been written into it yet.
key_provisions: No provisions written yet
Title reserved as a placeholder
Awaiting content from Minority Leader
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet since this bill has no content, but whoever the Minority Leader decides to write it for could be impacted once real policy is added.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder with no content and no action has been taken on it yet.

plain_title: A Bill Held Open for the Minority Leader to Name
plain_summary: This is essentially a placeholder bill — a spot being saved in Congress for the Minority Leader (the head of whichever party has fewer members) to introduce a bill later. No actual law or policy has been written into it yet. Think of it like a reserved seat at a table waiting for someone to sit down and order.
key_provisions: No provisions written yet
Title is reserved for future use
Minority Leader will determine the content
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet since the bill has no content, but once filled in, it could affect anyone depending on what the Minority Leader chooses to propose.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder and has no content or actions taken on it yet — it is waiting to be filled in by the Minority Leader.

plain_title: A Bill Held Open for the Minority Party Leader
plain_summary: This is a placeholder bill number that has been officially set aside for the Minority Leader of Congress to use when they are ready to introduce legislation. Think of it like saving a seat — the spot is reserved but no actual law or proposal has been written yet. Until the Minority Leader fills in the details, there is no specific policy or change being proposed.
key_provisions: Placeholder reserved for future use
No specific rules or changes proposed yet
Minority Leader will define the content later
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet because this bill has no content — it is simply a reserved slot waiting for a real proposal to be added.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a reserved number with no actual content or actions taken yet — it is essentially an empty placeholder.

plain_title: A Bill Placeholder Saved for the Minority Party Leader
plain_summary: This is essentially a reserved spot in the legislative queue set aside for the Minority Leader, who is the head of the party that does not control Congress. No actual bill text or proposal has been written yet. Think of it like a saved seat — the title exists, but the content hasn't been filled in.
key_provisions: No provisions written yet
Bill text has not been introduced
Reserved as a placeholder for future legislation

original_title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to provide certain line item veto authority to the President.
plain_title: Giving the President Power to Cut Specific Spending Items
plain_summary: This proposal would change the Constitution to give the President a special power called a 'line item veto,' which lets them reduce specific spending amounts in a budget bill instead of having to accept or reject the whole thing. For example, if Congress passed a budget with money for a project the President thought was wasteful, they could cut just that part. Congress can override the cut if two-thirds of both the House and Senate vote against it.
key_provisions: President can reduce specific spending amounts in budget bills
President must notify Congress within 10 days of making a cut
Congress can reverse the cut with a two-thirds vote in both chambers
who_it_affects: This would affect all Americans by shifting some budget control from Congress to the President, potentially changing how taxpayer money is spent on government programs.
status_plain_english: This proposal was sent to the House Judiciary Committee, where it will be reviewed before any further steps can happen — most bills that reach committee never make it further.

original_title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
plain_title: House Officially Notifies Senate of Its New Leadership
plain_summary: At the start of a new Congress, the House of Representatives sends an official message to the Senate letting them know that enough members have shown up to do business. This particular message also announces who was chosen as the two most important House officials: the Speaker (Mike Johnson) and the Clerk (Kevin McCumber). Think of it like a new school year where the class formally introduces its president and secretary to the rest of the school.
key_provisions: Confirms enough House members are present to officially begin work
Announces Mike Johnson as the elected Speaker of the House
Announces Kevin McCumber as the elected Clerk of the House
who_it_affects: This resolution mainly affects how Congress operates internally, signaling to the Senate and the public that the House is officially organized and ready to pass laws in the 119th Congress.
status_plain_english: This resolution has been fully approved and finalized — no further action is needed.

original_title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
plain_title: Telling the President That Congress Is Ready to Work
plain_summary: This resolution lets the House of Representatives officially inform the President that enough members of both the House and Senate have shown up to do their jobs. Think of it like taking attendance at the start of school — Congress needs to confirm that enough people are present before they can officially get started. This is a formal tradition that happens at the beginning of a new session of Congress.
key_provisions: Speaker of the House is allowed to pick a group to deliver the message
Both the House and Senate confirm they have enough members present
The President is officially notified that Congress is ready to begin
who_it_affects: This mainly affects members of Congress and the President, as it is an internal government procedure to officially kick off a new session of Congress.
status_plain_english: This resolution was agreed to and finalized without any objections, meaning it passed smoothly and no further action is needed.
